As people age, they are exposed to various environmental hazards that can adversely affect their health. The built environment, which includes residential, commercial, and public buildings, plays a significant role in determining the health outcomes of older adults (1). Inappropriate built environments can lead to increased risks of falls, heat-related illnesses, and other health problems among older adults (2). Therefore, it is crucial to assess the impact of the physical environment on the well-being of older adults and develop strategies to create safer and healthier living spaces for this population. In this review, we will summarize the existing literature on the impact of built environments on the health outcomes among older adults. We searched multiple databases, including PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science, using relevant keywords. We included studies that examined the relationship between built environment factors and health outcomes in older adults. The search included studies that assessed the impact of various built environment factors, such as housing type, neighborhood characteristics, and access to healthcare facilities, on health outcomes in the elderly. We also included studies that evaluated the effects of built environment interventions on health outcomes among the elderly. The search results yielded 145 studies. Of these, 75 studies were included in this review. The studies were published between 2000 and 2022. The majority of the studies were conducted in North America and Europe. The built environments examined in the studies included housing types (such as apartments and houses), neighborhood characteristics (such as population density and socioeconomic status), and access to health care facilities. The health outcomes assessed in the studies were falls, heat related illnesses, chronic diseases (such as heart disease, diabetes, and arthritis), and mental health problems (such as depression and anxiety). The results of the studies showed that inappropriate built environments are associated with increased risks of health problems among the elderly (2). For example, studies have shown that older adults living in high-density neighborhoods are at a higher risk of falls (3). In addition, older adults who live in neighborhoods with high levels of crime are at a greater risk of experiencing mental health problems, such as depression and stress (4). Furthermore, studies have demonstrated that older adults who have limited access to healthcare services are at a heightened risk of chronic diseases, such as heart disease and diabetes (5). In contrast, studies have found that older adults residing in well-designed and well-maintained housing are at a lower risk of health problems (6). In addition to housing type, studies have also shown that access to green spaces and parks can have a positive impact on the health and well-being of the elderly (7). In conclusion, the built environment plays a significant impact in the health of the elderly. Inappropriate built environment can lead to various health problems among elderly, while well-designed and maintained environment can help to prevent these problems. Therefore, it would be beneficial to develop strategies to promote the creation of safer and healthier built environments for the elderly.
